tribe_description:
This tribe buys like “lived-routine forecasters.” They assume most products can work in a clean, first-use scenario; what they are really testing is whether the product will still feel effortless once it has to survive real life: wet hands, cramped counters, travel, missing accessories, worn consumables, and the need to replace one part without re-buying the whole system. They treat a product as a chain of small rituals—charge, store, dock, clean, attach, swap, and source replacements—and they predict satisfaction by looking for the weak link that will quietly turn the item into a drawer resident.

Their central belief is continuity beats capability. Performance features matter, but only if the ownership loop stays coherent over time. That’s why they audit interfaces with almost forensic granularity: which charging standard each component uses, whether a kit mixes standards, and whether replacement heads/tips/consumables match a dominant ecosystem. Mixed standards are not “a minor annoyance” to them; they interpret the inconsistency itself as evidence of incoherent design thinking, which predicts downtime (lost-cable events), travel friction, and replacement scarcity later. When they praise a product, they often phrase it as ownership relief—“this matches the common ecosystem, so I’m not trapped”—and they’ll be specific about which consumable category is standardized versus locked-in.

They also treat mundane mechanics as truth-tellers. Reviews from this tribe don’t stop at “charging is awkward”; they describe the physical ritual required (how a cover is accessed, whether a tool is needed to pry something open, and the exact posture the device must sit in while charging). They see those micro-frictions as compounding costs that drive non-adoption even when core performance is fine. Similarly, bulkiness and unnatural handling aren’t framed as preferences—they are adoption predictors. If it’s annoying to hold, place, or manipulate repeatedly, they assume it won’t stay in the routine.

When evaluating kits and bundles, they refuse bundle-level verdicts. They split the purchase into separate products, benchmark each against a named baseline or category standard, then recombine the conclusion around the component that sets the ceiling on satisfaction. Their value logic is “the weakest link determines lived value,” so readers can tell which piece carries the purchase and which one will generate regret.

They write reviews as evidence-based ownership math. They verify listing claims through hands-on observations, elevate any spec-to-experience mismatch as a return predictor, and calibrate build concerns through concrete mechanical tells (wobble, looseness, fit tolerances) often compared against a known standard. When design implies extra intensity or risk, they don’t just warn; they infer a mechanism and give a practical start protocol, because they see reviewing as preventing avoidable bad first uses. You can predict their reviews will read like a field report on continuity: what will integrate cleanly, what will create future dependency, and which small physical choices will become daily friction later.
tribe_definition: A tribe of hands-on gear analysts who evaluate products based on what the label suggests. They generally prioritize hands-on use, equipment and gear.
population_definition: Gamers who evaluate video games, consoles, accessories, and entertainment products.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When evaluating a product, they break the listing into testable sub-claims and then try to confirm each with direct, hands-on observations. They treat unverified specs as provisional and elevate any spec-to-experience mismatch because they believe it predicts returns and long-term frustration. In reviews, they cite the specific observation that supports the conclusion (what physically changed, what failed, what could not be done in normal use) rather than relying on overall impressions.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes marketing claims are cheap and only observable evidence predicts ownership outcomes; gap blocks repeatedly showed actual reviews anchored conclusions in concrete physical evidence and called out claim/experience mismatches, while predictions stayed generic and lost the evidentiary move (missing_nuance, multiple gaps).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When trying daily-use gear, they evaluate the ownership loop as the real product: how it docks, charges, stores, cleans, and reassembles under normal conditions. They describe charging and storage as physical rituals with constraints (what must be opened, what position it must sit in, what gets awkward around sinks/counters), because they believe friction here compounds into non-use. In reviews, they will downgrade an item that performs well but forces awkward posture or placement during routine steps.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes small daily rituals decide adoption more than headline performance; gap blocks emphasized device-specific charging geometry/ritual as decisive, and this window adds that they often name the exact access method (including when a tool is needed to pry a cover) rather than vague ‘awkward charging’ language (HIGH, repeated).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When the purchase is a kit, bundle, or multi-function device, they evaluate each component as if bought alone before allowing the bundle to count as a deal. They benchmark each part against a named real-world baseline and state equivalence by dimension (as-good-as vs worse-than) rather than giving a single blended verdict. In reviews, they explicitly identify which component carries the value and which one sets the ceiling on satisfaction.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes bundles hide weak links, so fairness requires component-by-component baselining; gaps showed consistent decomposition and baseline mapping, and this window reinforces that they recombine into a verdict based on each component’s role rather than treating one item as ‘the main product’ (MEDIUM-HIGH, repeated).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When judging long-term value, they foreground replaceability and ecosystem fit as the main cost lever: what consumables wear out first, what standard they match, and how easy it will be to source equivalents later. They treat compatibility with a dominant ecosystem as immediate purchase relief and will say so plainly, because it changes the cost-to-own equation. In reviews, they call out the exact consumable category that is standardized versus locked-in, rather than offering generic ‘replacement parts are available’ reassurance.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes true value is cost-to-own, not price, and that ecosystem-standard consumables are the biggest lever; gaps showed they celebrated non-proprietary replacements when tied to a dominant ecosystem and were precise about which consumable category got that benefit—predictions misassigned the relief (MEDIUM-HIGH).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When a product uses proprietary charging or uncommon interfaces, they treat it as continuity risk rather than a convenience detail. They model concrete failure modes—lost cable, travel friction, inability to source replacements—and downgrade the product even if core performance is strong. If a kit mixes interface standards across its components, they escalate the critique because the inconsistency itself is taken as evidence of incoherent design and future serviceability trouble.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes interface coherence predicts long-term usability; multiple gaps (including this window) showed they audit standards per component, treat mixed standards as a design-coherence red flag, and tie it to downstream continuity failures—predictions reassigned the issue to the wrong component or flattened it (HIGH, repeated and salient).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When specs or geometry imply different real-world intensity or risk, they convert that inference into a usable protocol rather than leaving it as a vague caution. They explain the mechanism they think is responsible (pressure, seal, opening size, leverage) and give a practical starting procedure that reduces the chance of an unpleasant first session. In reviews, this shows up as step-by-step guidance meant to prevent avoidable discomfort and returns.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes responsible reviews prevent predictable misuse; a gap showed the actual review tied physical design dynamics to increased intensity and provided a specific start-setting protocol, while the prediction offered only generic warnings without mechanism (MEDIUM).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When comparing products, they use an equivalence-mapping mindset: what is truly the same, what is a downgrade, and what is an upgrade on a named axis. They expect trade-offs (bulk, complexity, proprietary parts) to be justified by clear functional gains that improve the lived routine, not just feature count. In reviews, they make the comparison structure explicit so readers can translate it to their own baseline.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes shoppers decide relative to a baseline, not in isolation; gaps around baseline brand comparisons and dimension-by-dimension scoring indicate explicit equivalence mapping, while predictions flattened this into standalone evaluation and generic adequacy language (MEDIUM).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When a product includes accessories, they judge them by scenario coverage—what specific problems each tip/attachment unlocks—rather than treating them as generic extras. They praise a set when distinct attachments meaningfully expand use for particular needs and call out when the box is padded with redundant pieces. In reviews, they name which attachments matter and why, because they believe accessory utility is part of honest value accounting.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes accessory value is functional coverage, not item count; gap evidence (including this window) showed accessory praise was tied to named attachments that expanded real scenarios, while predictions ignored the specific pieces that served as the reviewer’s positive evidence (MEDIUM).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When handling comfort and form factor are central to use, they treat ‘bulkiness’ and ‘unnatural to use’ as integration tests, not subjective nitpicks. They assume an awkward grip, size, or motion will reduce adherence and effectively erase performance advantages, so they foreground handling realism in the verdict. In reviews, they frame ergonomics as a keep-or-abandon predictor: if it doesn’t fit the routine comfortably, it won’t stay in the routine.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes adoption is behavioral, not aspirational; a gap showed the decisive veto was handling form factor as an integration failure, while the prediction substituted performance-consistency framing and omitted the usability veto signal (MEDIUM).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When they detect attachment wobble or looseness, they treat it as tolerance/QC evidence and calibrate it against a known comparison standard. Rather than speculating abstractly about durability, they describe how the fit differs from a baseline brand and what that implies for stability during use. In reviews, this becomes a predictive warning: small mechanical play today is read as an early indicator of future annoyance or failure.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes small mechanical ‘tells’ predict future reliability; gap blocks showed wobble was explicitly anchored to a baseline comparison and used as QC evidence, while predictions turned it into uncalibrated speculation and lost the comparative calibration (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: When buying tech or gear they’ll use repeatedly, they’re motivated by confidence that it will keep working with minimal surprise costs or lock-in. They prefer products that remain serviceable through standardized parts, replaceable attachments, and common interfaces, because that reduces future dependency on one brand. Their satisfaction comes from feeling ‘future-proofed’ as much as from day-one performance.
main.rationalization: Gaps indicate a strong underlying need to avoid ecosystem traps (cross-compatible consumables, mixed/proprietary charging treated as long-term risk) rather than merely optimizing upfront value (HIGH).

main.text: They’re driven to catch the hidden ‘daily friction’ costs that don’t show up in spec sheets—how charging, cleaning, docking, and part fit affect sustained use. They experience poor ergonomics and maintenance hassle as a form of product dishonesty because it shifts effort onto the buyer. This is why they spend review attention on mundane routines other shoppers skip.
main.rationalization: Repeated gap emphasis on device-specific charging geometry/ritual and handling integration showed routine friction was decisive, and this window reinforced the preference for concrete ritual details (e.g., when access requires prying) over abstract battery/build notes (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: They’re motivated by protecting other buyers through actionable guidance, not just opinions—turning observations into operating recommendations and warnings. When a design choice changes comfort or safety dynamics, they feel compelled to spell out how to use it correctly and what to avoid. They see this as peer-to-peer accountability that compensates for thin manufacturer guidance.
main.rationalization: Gap evidence showed translation of design physics into an explicit safe-use starting protocol, reflecting a helper/guardian motive beyond generic performance commentary (MEDIUM).

main.text: When choosing between similar options, they’re motivated by products that prove reliability through tight tolerances and coherent design choices rather than feature count. They treat wobble, loose fit, and inconsistent interface decisions as signs the maker cut corners in ways that will surface later as breakage or hassle. This drives them to emphasize mechanical ‘tell’ signs over polished marketing claims.
main.rationalization: Gaps showed wobble/looseness was used as a baseline-calibrated reliability signal and mixed charging standards were treated as a design red flag, while predictions glossed these as minor or missed them (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: When shopping for multi-part products, they’re motivated to avoid being misled by the convenience of bundling. They want to know which component meets category expectations and which one will create regret, because they view the weakest-link experience as what owners actually live with. This is why their reviews often read like two (or more) separate evaluations stitched into one value conclusion.
main.rationalization: Gaps repeatedly showed component-by-component benchmarking and explicit weakest-link accounting, and this window highlighted that they resist narratives that crown one component as ‘the main product’ when the other still needs category-equivalence scrutiny (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: When something feels awkward in the hand or bulky in the routine, they’re motivated to prevent ‘optimistic purchases’ that end up unused. They interpret form-factor friction as behavior-shaping: even strong performance won’t matter if the product is unpleasant to handle repeatedly. This makes them unusually sensitive to handling realism compared to spec-driven shoppers.
main.rationalization: A gap highlighted bulkiness/unnatural handling as the core adoption veto that predictions replaced with performance framing, indicating a strong avoidance of non-adoption purchases (MEDIUM).

implicit_goals.text: When selecting products for their setup, they aim to minimize long-term ownership friction—replacement parts, charging, and maintenance—so the item stays usable for years. They prefer purchases that won’t strand them when a cable is lost, a tip wears out, or a component breaks. This goal shows up in reviews as forward-looking warnings and ‘future-proofing’ advice tied to concrete failure modes.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gaps highlighted proprietary charging, mixed interface choices, and consumable compatibility as long-term risk levers that predictions ignored or mis-assigned, indicating an outcome focus on sustained usability (MEDIUM-HIGH).

implicit_goals.text: When reviewing bundled or multi-function products, they aim to give readers a component-by-component verdict anchored to real baselines so buyers can judge whether the bundle is truly a deal. They want peers to know which part is as-good-as a known standard, which part is worse-than, and where the real value is coming from. Their reviews are designed to prevent ‘bundle blindness’ where one strong component hides a weak companion device.
implicit_goals.rationalization: The divergence showed a clear goal of decomposing bundles and benchmarking each component against named standards, and this window reinforced that they assign roles to each component rather than letting one dominate the narrative (MEDIUM-HIGH).

implicit_goals.text: When they discover a spec-to-experience mismatch that could affect comfort or safety, they aim to convert it into practical user instructions other shoppers can follow immediately. They explain why the product behaves the way it does and then give a ‘start here’ procedure that reduces the learning curve. They want the next buyer to avoid the unpleasant first-use experiment they had to run.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gap showed they tied physical mechanism to intensity and provided a concrete starting protocol, beyond generic ‘be careful’ language (MEDIUM).

implicit_goals.text: When choosing long-lasting, high-performance products, they aim to ensure the product integrates smoothly into their existing setup—physically, ergonomically, and infrastructurally. They look for coherent interface choices, stable placement during charging/storage, and handling comfort that survives repetition. In reviews, they frame integration as the difference between a device they keep and one they abandon despite decent core performance.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Window gaps centered on charging workflow realism, mixed-interface incoherence, and bulk/handling fit as decisive integration criteria rather than abstract performance (MEDIUM).

implicit_goals.text: When writing reviews, they aim to provide guidance that predicts ownership outcomes rather than just reporting first impressions. They emphasize the failure modes that matter later—compatibility, replaceability, routine friction—and back them with observable evidence and baseline comparisons. Their desired outcome is a reader who can decide with confidence whether the product will stay in a routine or become a drawer item.
implicit_goals.rationalization: The gaps consistently show a predictive, failure-mode orientation (continuity risk, component benchmarking, ritual mechanics) that predictions flattened into generic praise/complaint (MEDIUM).

implicit_goals.text: When shopping, they aim to avoid costly mistakes by uncovering hidden trade-offs a listing won’t highlight, especially those that compound over time. They treat small design inconsistencies (like mixed standards or loose tolerances) as early warning signals and make the downstream consequence explicit. In reviews, they translate those signals into what will likely annoy, break, or cost money later.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gap evidence showed they escalated mixed interface choices, charging rituals (including prying/tool access), and wobble into long-horizon consequences and calibrated warnings, not generic annoyance notes (MEDIUM).

AHZGQXTGR3WB6CQR3PP2TB2YPTUA.product_description: KOFIRE UG-06 Wireless Gaming Headset for PS4 PS5 PC, 48H Playtime, Low Latency, Noise Cancelling Over Ear Bluetooth Gaming Headphones with Dual Mic, 3D Surround Sound, RGB Lighting
AHZGQXTGR3WB6CQR3PP2TB2YPTUA.review_text:
This headset comes with Bluetooth 5.0 version, which is the latest Bluetooth technology at this time. The connection was exceptionally steady, and lag-free audio of low latency within 40ms, which is relatively low compared to other wireless headphones in the price range.
Being a fast-paced gaming enthusiast, you must know how a high latency can ruin your gameplay. Therefore, I have always preferred wired headphones due to their latency, but the UG-06 completely changed my perception of wireless headphones.
I often get nervous while playing using a wired gaming headset due to the cable. It rolls around the chair, and as an aggressive gamer (not proud of) with uncontrollable hand movements, I often end up pulling the cord out of the 3.5mm jack. With the UG-06 headphone, playing games is a breeze, and I have more room for hand movements.
The UG-06 comes with beautiful RGB light, which is one of its major attractions besides its sleek black body. I don’t use the RGB lighting much, so I often turn them off while playing. I only turn them for 2-3 hours at night time. Right now, the headphone gives 30 hours of playtime at a single charge. The good thing is that even at a low charge, I didn’t find any compromise in sound quality, which is often a problem in many wireless headsets.
Even if your charge goes off, you can connect the 3.5mm audio cable to the headset and use it as a wired headset. The only problem is that you won’t be able to use the RGB lights and mute function while using the wired connection. Even with RGB lights off, visual appearance is really cool. They have this shiny hematite looking detail covering the ear cups (kinda like black mirror) giving it cool and futuristic look. You can see on the pictures and the video I am also uploading. Furthemore, cups are padded with super soft padding making them really comfortable. Headband is also nicely padded and adjustable, it doesn’t squeeze my brains out after longer use.
Coming to quality, I am in awe of its 3D immersive sound effects. I played Horizon Forbidden West, and I cannot praise it enough for its 50 mm super-size audio driver that provides a broader sound field and an ultra-immersive gaming experience.
Pros
• Steady Connection & Low Latency for lag-free sound in fast-paced games
• Adjustable headband and soft fully-enclosing earmuff for superior grip and all-day comfort
• 30 hours of continuous playtime
• Dual microphone with noise canceling feature
• Crystal-clear calls with HD voice during games
• 7 Colorful RGB Light on both sides with automatic color changing loop

AHDAHLSCUDCFZD35S6IPW5QZ5NHA.product_description: The intergalactic adventurers are back with a bang. Help them stop a robotic Emperor intent on conquering cross-dimensional worlds, with their own universe next in the firing line. Blast your way home with an arsenal of outrageous weaponry. Experience the Shuffle of dimensional Rifts and dynamic gameplay. Explore never-before-seen planets and alternate dimension versions of old favorites. PS5 features: feel in-game actions through the haptic feedback of the dual sense wireless Controller. Take full control of advanced weapon mechanics, made possible by adaptive triggers. Planet-hop at hyper-speed via the near-instant loading of the PS5 console's SSD. Immerse your ears with Tempest 3D audio Tech as you work to save the universe. Enhanced lighting and rays tracking render dazzling in-game worlds - displayed in crisp, dynamic 4K and HDR. Choose performance mode to enjoy targeted 60 frames per second gameplay. (With compatible headphones. Dynamic 4K and HDR require a compatible 4K and HDR compatible TV or display.)
AHDAHLSCUDCFZD35S6IPW5QZ5NHA.review_text:
I have played every mainline Ratchet & Clank game since the first one in 2002. I think that's 11 Ratchet games total that I've played. I'll admit I skipped out on Full Frontal Assault, though. I'm definitely what I would consider a lover of the series. Rift Apart is absolutely beautiful and has some of the best visuals I've seen out of a console. The gameplay is very familiar and feels exactly like the more modern Ratchet games with some fantastic additions with the PS5's DualSense controller. The adaptive triggers, the new haptics, and the mini speaker really makes the crazy weapons feel impactful.

Let's get this out of the way, the new playable Lombax, Rivet is beyond cute. I was surprised to find Jennifer Hale voices the character and honestly, she puts James Arnold Taylor (who has been voicing Ratchet for 20 years) to shame. Her portrayal of the character is dead-on perfect. I won't dig into the story here as it should be experienced as it's played, but the story is simple and predictable. The characters have reasonable and meaningful arcs, but it honestly feels a bit shallow. Dr. Nefarious returns as the primary antagonist and he's as ridiculous and over-the-top as always. I miss Lawrence.

My biggest criticism is that Ratchet and Rivet play absolutely identically. Other than the character on screen there's absolutely no difference in how they play. They have the same weapons and gadgets available and the same move-set, although they have unique animations. Some variety in gameplay would be nice when switching between them. Secondly, and less of a complaint, is the lack of optional objectives in the worlds. Ratchet & Clank has been known for creating sprawling levels with multiple paths that take you to unique objectives, hidden gear, or new gadgets. While they're definitely present here, they're not nearly as long/open/sprawling as in some previous games. I suspect the development cycle on this game was significantly shorter than in other titles, probably owing to this being a launch-window title for PS5 and for Insomniac also developing TWO Spider-Man games at the same time. It's forgivable, but I was expecting more depth to the planets and levels.

Where the game truly shines is visuals and combat, which go hand-in-hand here. I played on the Performance RT mode. Gameplay is incredibly fluid, fast-paced and allows for a lot of traversal options. The new weapons are a pleasant mix of familiar and new. Though there seems to be a larger emphasis on crowd-control and un-aimed weapons. The visuals are absolutely top-notch with incredibly detailed character models. Ratchet, Clank and Rivet look particularly stunning. Clank has ray-traced reflections on his shiny surfaces which can really help the immersion and feeling that he's a little metal dude. Battle sequences can be visually messy, but the colors and telegraphed attacks help to mitigate the "diarrhea Christmas Lights" that can come from overwhelming chaos on screen.

Overall, it's an absolutely great game. It's not the best Ratchet & Clank game, but it's really good. Fans will love it as it keeps the story going (taking place after PS3s Into the Nexus), references past events, and introduces new elements. Rivet totally steals the show and I'm totally on board for her inevitable solo game/series.

AHDAHLSCUDCFZD35S6IPW5QZ5NHA.product_description: Join Aloy as she braves the Forbidden West - a majestic but dangerous frontier that conceals mysterious new threats. Explore distant lands, fight bigger and more awe-inspiring machines, and encounter astonishing new tribes as you return to the far-future, post-apocalyptic world of Horizon. The land is dying. Vicious storms and an unstoppable blight ravage the scattered remnants of humanity, while fearsome new machines prowl their borders. Life on Earth is hurtling towards another extinction, and no one knows why. It's up to Aloy to uncover the secrets behind these threats and restore order and balance to the world. Along the way, she must reunite with old friends, forge alliances with warring new factions and unravel the legacy of the ancient past - all the while trying to stay one step ahead of a seemingly undefeatable new enemy.
AHDAHLSCUDCFZD35S6IPW5QZ5NHA.review_text:
Horizon II: Forbidden West is a great sequel, but it's a bit heavy-handed and potentially burdened with making sure everything from the first game is bigger and more robust. It does a lot of things really well and overall it's a very fun, enjoyable, and even emotional game. But it also gets in it's own way and has a number of systems at work that, in my opinion, just clog up the moment-to-moment gameplay. It's a good open-world game that, in my opinion, doesn't need to be open. If told as a linear story with tight pacing I think the game would SHINE. But, open-world RPG is popular, so here it is.

This game takes off shortly after the events of Horizon: Zero Dawn and sets you on a quest of ridding the world of a 'blight' that is killing local fauna and is rapidly spreading. That short-term goal is pretty quickly set aside for a much larger and wildly unexpected plot. Aloy finds new allies and reunites with old allies to combat a new threat, all while doing the shoot-robot-dinosaurs-in-an-open-world thing.

The new "Forbidden West" is a fantastic environment. The map size is large, and honestly I think too large for its own good. It's got a lot of variety in it, however. Desert plains, snowy mountain ranges, lush jungles, rough ocean water, sunken cities, redwood forests; it's very well represented as a smash-up of everything west of the Rockies. The game looks beautiful on PS5 (I played in 'performance' mode). I'm primarily a PC gamer but keep a PlayStation around for the exclusives, and this really does live up to some of the best fidelity I've seen on PC in recent years. Character models are great and I really like how well represented and diverse they are, with characters having real skin flaws and believable texture.

Speaking of characters, they have improved the most since the original Horizon game. Characters have much more development, their own arcs, worthwhile dialog, and real motivations. It's quite clear that they've been motion captured and face captured for their performances this time around with a lot of expressiveness coming through during animation. Zero Dawn's animation and cutscenes were stiff and rudimentary; this is drastically improved with Forbidden West and I genuinely liked interacting with my allies because they felt like real friends. Over the course of the game you end up building a 'crew' and working out of a 'base' -- it actually felt a lot like Mass Effect and the Normandy.

The main plot has some twists and turns, but follows a familiar formula. "Go here, get these things, build the base up, build your team up, get the bad guy, something goes wrong, rally your team, get the bad guy for real this time." There's a pretty significant revelation in the first quarter or so of the game that really shifts the tone and resets the stakes.

The gameplay hasn't changed since the first game. There are a handful of new weapon types, some I liked and others I didn't. Upgrading weapons, armors, and pouches is handily the the worst mechanic in the game. It feels monotonous and uninspired, especially since some materials required for the crafting are found only in certain sections of the game. This may require a ridiculous amount of backtracking or fast-traveling and running around to find that ONE rabbit that drops the ONE pelt you need. An upgrade system with a single 'currency' would be more straightforward and preferable. Combat remains similar to Zero Dawn, but there's a little more variation in the way encounters can be approached. Ultimately it always comes down to shooting enemies over-and-over until they go down and doing lots of silly-looking dodge rolls while healing. There's new elemental attacks as well, with certain enemies being more 'strong' or 'weak' to certain elements. And unless you can remember all 30+ enemies and what their strength and weakness are you're going to be 'scanning' them A LOT.

I put about 40 hours into the game to complete it and left a lot of side quests unfinished and lots of areas of the map unexplored, mostly because I was becoming bored and wanted to advance the story (the best part of the game, really) to see it unfold. It's easy to out-level the story content if you do a reasonable amount of side objectives. I encountered just a couple of bugs, only one of which actually broke progression and I had to restart a save. More often I was just frustrated with things like combat mechanics and climbing mechanics that didn't behave like I was expecting or wanted to. My absolute biggest gripe actually comes from Aloy's internal monologue; this woman talks to herself (or you, the player) CONSTANTLY and is giving hints to puzzles or environmental obstacles before I've even had a chance to assess them. The hand-holding and tutorialization is radically excessive. Overall I really enjoyed my time with Horizon II: Forbidden West. The story is honestly quite good and well-written, leaving a very clear set-up for a sequel. It's absolutely worth playing; it's a great game but has some issues.

AHDAHLSCUDCFZD35S6IPW5QZ5NHA.product_description: Relive the beloved game that started it all – rebuilt for the PlayStation®5 console. Experience the emotional storytelling and unforgettable characters in The Last of Us™, winner of over 200 Game of the Year awards. In a ravaged civilization, where infected and hardened survivors run rampant, Joel, a weary protagonist, is hired to smuggle 14-year-old Ellie out of a military quarantine zone. However, what starts as a small job soon transforms into a brutal cross-country journey. Includes the complete The Last of Us single-player story and celebrated prequel chapter, Left Behind, which explores the events that changed the lives of Ellie and her best friend Riley forever.
AHDAHLSCUDCFZD35S6IPW5QZ5NHA.review_text:
The headline review might seem over-the-top, but I truly believe that The Last of Us is the best video game that's ever been made, to date (that I've played). This version of the game has been rebuilt (and not just remastered) and takes everything from the 2013 release cleans it up, and polishes it for the PS5 platform. It's the EXACT same game, the EXACT same character performances, and the EXACT same gameplay that's wrapped up in a much prettier and better-performing package.

Playing The Last of Us: Part I is an odd experience for somebody that's played the original 2013 release and the 2014 remastered release. It's exactly how I REMEMBER the 2013 release looking and feeling, but it's truly better in every measurable way. The gameplay is unchanged, but a higher framerate and cleaner visuals with increased resolution make it feel great. The powerful and emotional story scenes play out exactly the same as the 2013 release but with higher detail character models the emotion on display is remarkable and those heavy scenes hit harder. The UI and HUD has been updated and is in line with The Last of Us: Part II.

Additional options are available here as well. A lot of accessibility features and customization are readily available making this game more approachable to more people. HDR is also available for those with capable displays. I will admit that I had issues getting HDR to work properly for awhile; and it eventually just 'fixed itself' one day. I would get a message on startup telling me I had an HDR display and asking if I wanted to switch to the HDR, but would then tell me I had an incompatible display. It worked beautifully once it decided it work, though. I played in performance mode for a 60fps experience and never experienced frame drops or stutter. PS5 features like adaptive triggers and 'high-definition' rumble are actually incredibly immersive. Raindrops will ever-so-gently pitter-patter in your hands. Horses hooves clip-clop with surprisingly detailed feeling. Pulling a bowstring tighter increases trigger resistance and makes for a more 'connected' feeling with your on-screen actions.

The game itself is what I would truly consider a masterpiece, both in 2013 and in 2022. Nothing is out-of-place. Every performance is stellar. Every moment works in service of the characters and the story. The story itself is grim, dark, lighthearted, frustrating, and beautiful. The soundtrack is and always has been beautifully haunting. This is a game that showcases that video games are truly an art form.

There is a lot of dispute as to whether this game justifies a $70 price tag. Nobody but you can decide what value personally means. I offer my opinion on it's price and value; The Last of Us: Part I is a whole-newly built game using the framework of an already great game. It's like restoring a classic car; the developers have done a complete restoration and improved what could be improved without compromising the vision. There's a lot of talent behind what was done to rebuild the game and I feel the price is justified. It's $10 more than what I paid for in 2013. I've spent $10 on things far, far stupider. Some folks will complain that the multiplayer component is missing from this release. I personally could not care, I never did and never will play the multiplayer components so the absence is not relevant to my interests. I genuinely do not believe this is a 'cash-grab' release, but I also don't doubt in the least that it's fantastic advertising for the upcoming HBO adaptation.

This game is an absolute must-play for those that have the slightest interest. If you've never played The Last of Us this is your time. If you've played The Last of Us or the 2014 remaster and you enjoyed it, you'll enjoy it all over again, but perhaps you want to wait for a sale.

AGC3AAQ4MVORRK3QWVIQ5ZM4LM4A.product_description: A revolutionary way to play your Nintendo Switch! Enjoy the full-size controller experience in handheld mode with the HORI Split Pad Pro. Featuring full-size analog sticks, a precision D-pad, and large shoulder buttons designed for comfort and accuracy, even during marathon gaming sessions. Advanced features include programmable rear triggers, Turbo functionally, assignable buttons, and more. Perfect for high-pace action titles such as Daemon X Machina and many other Nintendo Switch titles. Enhance your gaming experience with the Split Pad Pro! Officially Licensed by Nintendo. (Not a wireless controller - only for handheld mode. Does not include Motion Controls, HD Rumble, NFC, or IR camera. NOTE: If having trouble with the Switch recognizing Split Pad Pro, please ensure that all Joy-Cons are off and not connected via Bluetooth.)
AGC3AAQ4MVORRK3QWVIQ5ZM4LM4A.review_text:
If you've got big hands or have struggled to play the Switch with JoyCons, then you know why you're looking at these controllers. You probably will appreciate them, but there are some pros/cons/drawbacks you should be aware of.

You're gonna see a lot of reviews all repeating the same things about this accessory. I make mention of those as well, but I also felt the need to write my review because there are a number of important notes that NO ONE else is mentioning, so here goes!

PROS:
- Big, full-sized buttons and analog sticks
- More comfortable button/stick layout due the wideness (the sticks aren't directly below the buttons)
- Provide a better grip than JoyCons (though not perfect, read more below)
- Concave analog sticks (this might be subjective)
- Plug & Play; they connect immediately and easily
- Customizable "Assign" button on each side (with one caveat read below)
- Customizable "Turbo" button on each side (with same caveat, read below)
- They have 'memory' so your "Assign" buttons stay assigned even when the system is turned off
- Switch fits in the dock with them attached

CONS:
- They DRAIN THE CONSOLE'S BATTERY /!\ (Read more below) (My biggest complaint and disappointment)
- "Assign" buttons can't be set to (+) or (-)
- Lack of Motion Controls
- Lack of vibration feedback
- Lack of Amiibo support
- The wideness of the system might feel weird, at least at first (read more in caveats)
- Good luck finding a carrying case to fit these!

CAVEATS:
- The grip is not as perfect as I'd hoped. It's still good and definitely better than the JoyCons, however I was hoping for something with more to hold onto in the palm. I think these would have benefitted from being a little more functional and aesthetic in this regard. They sure look cool, but they'd be better to hold onto if the "handle" parts stuck out a bit more and were more rounded/bulbous than being a short little arrow head. I still find myself having to support the device with my pinkies or on my lap/abdomen because of this.
- Since they are two independent controllers that don't actually communicate with each other, the Turbo and Assign buttons can only be assigned to a button on its respective side. In other words, you can't set the left Assign button to A, B, X, Y, R, or ZR and you can't set the right Assign button to Up, Down, Left, Right, L, or ZL. This is an important drawback if you were hoping to set the extra buttons to Left and Right, or multiple face buttons. It's not a total deal-breaker, but something to be aware of if you had specific games/gameplay in mind.
- The Assign buttons also CANNOT BE SET TO (+) OR (-)! This is a downer for me. I'm not sure why they did this. I doubt there is any technical limitation; so they probably wrongly assumed that no one would want quick access to the tings that are usually assigned to these buttons (maps and menus), but this was a very poor decision IMO. A lot of games use + or - for menus and maps and it would have been awesome to have a game's map at hand like that.
- Since these are so wide, they do make holding the system a bit odd at first. I think you might get used to it. I've only had one play session so far, so I'm not sure, but it did seem to become less or unnoticeable by the time I stopped playing.

SUBJECTIVE NOTES:
- Very lightweight, more so than the JoyCons. This can be a positive or negative depending on preference.
- I find concave analog sticks to be much better on every controller ever and would burn convex sticks from history if I could, but I understand that some insane people prefer those for some crazy reason, lol.
- The Turbo buttons have two modes that can be cycled through: Set a turbo button once and it will be turbo only while you hold the button, set that button as turbo a second time and that button will autofire without pressing it at all. Personally, I find this a bit annoying because you can't quickly unset the turbo without having the button get spammed for a moment while you cycle it twice. This may or may not be any issue to you, and I can imagine the feature would be useful for certain games like shoot-em-ups, space shooters, bullet hell, etc.

ON THE SUBJECT OF AMIIBO/VIBRATION/MOTION (My Opinion):
- It is important to be aware that these features are missing, but how important they are to you will greatly depend on what games you play and how you wish to play them.
- Amiibo support really isn't a big deal, as they aren't super useful in games and generally are only used for "bonus" type things; never a main mechanic you need often at all. Also, since these controllers are intended for handheld play, you're probably carrying them around with you, maybe outside of home, in which case, you're probably not lugging around a bunch of bulky plastic figures as well.
- Lack of rumble is too bad, but it's not a necessary feature for the majority of games. I think there could be some games that need "HD Rumble" for things, but I'm not sure in this mode. The only two games I can think of that use HD Rumble as a mechanic are 1-2 Switch and some Mario Party minigames. The former can only be played with the JoyCons attached, so it's a non-issue. Mario Party has the same situation if you're playing with a group of people. If you're playing solo, go play a better solo game! Lol, kidding, but I'm really not sure how those minigames work in handheld mode when playing solo even with real JoyCons.
- Lack of motion control is the biggest of these three drawbacks if you ask me, as they are often very useful or even necessary in some games such as Breath of the Wild (aiming arrows and such) and Splatoon (you don't NEED to play Splatoon with motion controls, but most players do and those of us who do know how much better it is to play with them). I really wish they'd have put this feature in. Maybe they'll make an upgraded version with it some day.

ABOUT THE CONSOLE BATTERY DRAIN:
Because these don't have any internal battery like official JoyCons do, they are basically have to sap power from the system (the power transfer is the same way JoyCons charge via the console when it's docked). I could be wrong, but I'm pretty sure that's how it has to work - because they need power and that power must come from somewhere.

I've had a few play sessions with these. It's not quite a scientific test, but during those sessions, it was very apparent that my console battery was draining much faster than it usually does in handheld mode. Again, note that this is not a controlled scientific test or anything (I might go ahead and do a real controlled test sometime soon to verify), but it's pretty apparent and like I said, the controllers NEED to be powered somehow and the power draw mechanism IS there to charge JoyCons, so I'm near certain the Split Pad Pro saps power from the console while in use.

Sadly, this is a huge drawback for me and my biggest complaint. Ultimately this is what makes this device a disappointment for me and likely why I won't use them as often as I'd intended to. I REALLY WISH Hori had just put batteries in these things. How expensive could that be? Personally, I'd have paid an extra $10 easily to avoid this issue.

ABOUT PORTABLE/CARRYING CASE SITUATION:
This might change in time, but right now, I think you'll be hard-pressed to find a carrying case that can fit your Switch tablet and these in it. There's obviously no cases made specifically for the Switch that will fit with the system with them connected. You might be able to find some long generic carrying case like for a gun or something with standard insulating foam in it, but otherwise, you'll have to find one of those thicc phatboi cases that has large enough storage on the upper or lower compartment that will fit the Split Pad Pro. I have one case that is fairly big/tall (like 2.5 or 3 times as tall as the Switch, but it still can't fit these in it. I do believe I've seen taller ones than that available, so there's probably a solution, but you'll have to do a bunch of checking measurements and possibly even purchasing trial-and-error (thankfully Amazon does have free returns on a lot of items for Prime members now though!) though. Even then, though, remember now you're carrying a huge box around, lol. But I suppose that's the only way to take these out of the house.

I hope HORI or some other brand or Chinese generic accessory maker sees the opportunity and makes a case specifically for the Switch and these.

SUMMARY:
At the end of the day, if you've got big hands and want to play handheld, there is no other solution that provides a true controller layout like the Split Pad Pro does. There are some really good and well-reviews grips and grip cases out there, some that do provide better palm grip than the Split Pad Pro, however, none of them can make the JoyCon buttons and sticks larger or adjust their placement. However, the battery drain is a HUGE issue especially if you intend to use them to play outside. If you find the tiny buttons and sticks on the JoyCons difficult to use or that the squeezed together layout is uncomfortable and don't mind the battery drain or intend to play mostly indoors with access to a power supply, then you should definitely give the Split Pad a try. There's nothing like it!

If you play mostly outside and/or can't accept shortened console battery life, I'd advise you stay away.

If you're okay with the size and layout of the JoyCon buttons, but feel like you just need more grip in the palm area, then these may help, although the grip accessories and/or grip cases available might be a better solution for you. If you've got the money, I would suggest trying multiple things and deciding what feels best. I think most of those grips and cases on Amazon have free returns for Prime members, and I assume once the Split Pad Pro is restocked and they get it into the Prime warehouses, it may have the same situation, so you could try them all and return what's unneeded. Best of luck, fellow big-handed gamers!

AGAELWMVSVXNGXG7LNL4VCP6345Q.product_description: Dissidia Final Fantasy NT introduces squad-based battle gameplay by allowing you to wage war with over 20 of your favorite Final Fantasy characters. Combining unparalleled visuals, seamless gameplay, and your favorite characters from the past 30 years of the franchise, Dissidia Final Fantasy NT welcomes all gamers with 3 vs. 3 battles and the series renowned “bravery combat system”. AGAELWMVSVXNGXG7LNL4VCP6345Q.review_text: I regret buying this game. The tutorials are endless but still leave you not knowing how to play.

AGAELWMVSVXNGXG7LNL4VCP6345Q.product_description: From legendary creator Kaiju Infauna and the makers of Metroid Prime comes the “Rescore Definitive Edition”, an action-adventure masterfully crafted for a new generation. You are “Joule Adams”, one of the last remaining humans on the planet “Far Eden”, a mysterious and dangerous world controlled by robotic foes bent on your destruction. To survive you must forge friendships with a courageous group of “Courbet” companions, each with unique abilities and powers. The Rescore Definitive Edition includes the new “Eye of Obsidian” adventure. Join the plucky Courbet “Violet” and journey into the permanent midnight of the “Starving Sea”, a previously unexplored region of Far Eden where ancient enemies plot their revenge-on Joule and all humankind!
AGAELWMVSVXNGXG7LNL4VCP6345Q.review_text:
The "Definitive Edition" is a free update. If you have the original, there's no reason to buy again.

The game is very fun the first dozen hours or so, but the latter half of the main game consists of a large area without much to do, and a final dungeon whose length is probably more than half the game with an insane difficulty spike and a two-minute ending with very little payoff for a story that started off great. I haven't tried the definitive edition content yet, but it doesn't sound like it adds anything to improve the ending.

AGAELWMVSVXNGXG7LNL4VCP6345Q.product_description: Voted Windows Central 2018 Best Gaming Controller of the Year! Hyperkin has recreated the original Xbox controller aka "Duke" as an officially Xbox-licensed controller, compatible with the Xbox One family of devices and Windows 10 PCs. The Hyperkin Duke for Xbox One - Wired Controller features the original Xbox startup screen animated inside the Xbox Button, precision analog triggers, bumpers, a 3.5 mm headset jack, a 9 ft. detachable USB cable, and the return of the Black (RB) and White (LB) buttons. AGAELWMVSVXNGXG7LNL4VCP6345Q.review_text:
Absolutely everything you’d expect. I never owned an original Xbox, so i can’t compare this to the original, but the one bad thing I can say is the plastic isn’t quite the quality of 360 and Xbox Ones or PS2 or 4. Feels like a third party controller, but a good one.

However, it’s still excellent and I stand by my 5 stars. I was worried about getting used to the white and black buttons, but it DOES have bumpers! In addition the Home button is a tiny LED screen that plays the startup video of the original Xbox, so that’s neat. Except it does it every time you hit the button, not just on startup.

Very satisfied with my purchase.

AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.product_description: Water Dental Flosser Cordless for Teeth - Nicwell 4 Modes Dental Oral Irrigator, Portable and Rechargeable IPX7 Waterproof Powerful Battery Life Water Teeth Cleaner Picks for Home Travel
AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.review_text:
--Toothbrush

Toothbrush is as good as Sonic Care. Way better than manual toothbrush, get that dentist feeling clean if you’ve never used it before.

May have less options on strength and pulse variety depending on what version of Sonic Care you have.

Uses same head attachment as Sonic Care. Glad there’s no propriety brush head. Very easy to purchase more when you run out.

Charges on bottom via USB Type C. Cannot stand straight when charging. Must pull out rubber water resistant cover with pen or coin, and lay it on it’s side to charge.

--Water Flosser

Water flosser is like a portable WaterPik, but is bulky and not as natural to use. The orthodontic and tongue cleaner tips are a very nice addition.

Water pressure is a bit stronger than standard WaterPik head due to the small opening. Recommend you put it on Soft setting first

Head attachment is a bit loose compared to WaterPik. Wobbles when you touch it slightly.

Propriety head and charger. Have no idea why they didn’t go for USB Type C like for the toothbrush

Overall, very decent bundle. Toothbrush is very similar to Sonic Care brand but has odd charging method. Water flosser a bit strong, and must use propriety changing cable (careful not to lose it)
AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.best_prediction_review:
This kit ended up being a “two products in one box” situation for me. The water flosser itself does the job: on the first few uses it was obvious it’s getting along the gumline and between tight contacts better than a rinse, and it’s consistent night to night. It’s also stronger than I expected, and the smaller opening on the standard tip seems to concentrate the stream—start on Soft unless you want a rude first session, then step up once you know how it behaves.

Where I’m less impressed is the day-to-day routine stuff. The head on mine has a bit of wobble when seated; it still works, but that kind of loose fit reads like “this will get worse over time.” Tip compatibility is a big deal for me, and I was relieved it takes the common-style replacement tips (not some weird one-off you can’t source later).

Charging is the other mixed bag: the flosser is USB‑C, but the toothbrush in the bundle uses a proprietary cable. That inconsistency is exactly how you end up with one device dead because you lost the wrong cord. On top of that, the rubber charge cover is tight and the unit can’t really stand nicely while charging, so it tends to lay on its side on the counter.

Net: strong cleaning performance and good power, but the charging/fit details keep it from feeling “set and forget.”

AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.product_description: The sci-fi survival horror classic Dead Space returns, completely rebuilt from the ground up to offer a deeper and more immersive experience. This remake brings jaw-dropping visual fidelity, suspenseful atmospheric audio, and improvements to gameplay while staying faithful to the original game’s thrilling vision. Isaac Clarke is an everyman engineer on a mission to repair a vast mining ship, the USG Ishimura, only to discover something has gone horribly wrong. The ship's crew has been slaughtered and Isaac’s beloved partner, Nicole, is lost somewhere on board. Now alone and armed with only his engineering tools and skills, Isaac races to find Nicole as the nightmarish mystery of what happened aboard the Ishimura unravels around him. Trapped with hostile creatures called Necromorphs, Isaac faces a battle for survival, not only against the escalating terrors of the ship but his own crumbling sanity. System Requirements: Operating System: Windows 10 Operating System: Windows 10 Steam account required to download and install.
AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.review_text:
Remake of Dead Space (2008) game. Not a complete remake like (Resident Evil 2 and 3, or Final Final Fantasy Remake). The ship layout and core gameplay is similar. Mostly some updated visuals and some quality of life additions. There is added story with more dialogue and a new crew member, Kellion who gives commentary on what’s happening through coms.

Wished they updated the core gameplay. Very annoying when you have to change stances to melee, scan area. Don’t you why you can’t just shot then melee right after. The extra step/button press does build tension, but in terms of gameplay fluidity is feels unnecessary.

Game is linear for the most part, but does have a lot of backtracking and side missions. Must find keys/batteries to reach previous inaccessible areas

I had a okay time with it. A lot of great refinements to make it more modern

--List of Changes:

1. Updated visuals

Much higher detail in character models and environments

Way better lighting and particle effects

There’s added color instead of mostly grey and brown color palette

Redone Issac’s model. He’s shorter and put on a little more weight (looks bulkier). Also takes off his helmet form tim-to-time

2. More dialogue

In addition to the new crew member, Issac is no longer a silent protagonist and has some lines

3. No loading screens.

There are some artificial background loading screens when waiting for data to transfer or door to depressurize

4. New objectives / nodes implementation

Some mission objectives have changed (core story remains the same). For example instead of killing mid boss for a key, you have to do some puzzle/re-route power/oxygen from circuit breaker instead.

As you get further on story, you get security clearance to locked areas this time instead of using node resources. Nodes are stickly used for upgrades. The upgrade tree doesn’t have any blank nodes (each upgrade path use ultilized). Nodes are more rare this time around due to these changes

If you get lost and can’t figure out what to do, you must use guides for remake and not the original

5. Guns tied to story

Guns are unlocked while you progress through the story instead of searching for blueprints or schematics. Still need schematics to upgrade weapons

6. Free flow gravity segments

Now able to move wherever you want in gravity segments instead of pointing at area and launch off (superman swimming pool style). Gives more exploration to the levels, but also higher chance of getting lost ??

7. Side missions

New added side missions. More for getting new items, collectables than story. Side mission tracking has similar UI to Dead Space 3

8. Backtrack anytime*

Except for ending portion of the game, you can backtrack anytime for collectables and side missions. In original game once you finish a chapter you’re are locked out of content when moving onto the next chapter. This is no longer the case

9. Haptic Feedback on PS5 Controller

Mostly done in beginning (for first impressions) then it drops off more as you progress. Similar to GOW Ragnarök

10. Accessibility options

Auto-aim, stick sensitivity/dead zones, auto qte, show warning when pop up scare is about to arrive, toggle to sprint instead of hold, subtitle text customization, color blind mode, etc

11. New secret ending

Done by clearing New game +

AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.product_description: Adventure through Dream Land with Kirby and his allies—solo or with friends! The tough puff Kirby is back for a 1-4 player* platforming adventure across Planet Popstar. Copy enemies’ abilities and use their power to breathe fire, attack with a sword, wield a whip, and more! Shoot lasers and kick up a sandstorm with the brand-new Mecha and Sand Copy Abilities. Sometimes you’ll even need to unleash a powerful Super Ability to clear the way. In multiplayer, your friends can choose from Kirby, King Dedede, Meta Knight, and Bandana Waddle Dee as you float along and find Magolor’s missing ship pieces. Visit Merry Magoland and play a colorful collection of subgames Travel from Planet Popstar to Merry Magoland with the press of a button to share the fun with up to 3 additional friends as you compete on the same system*! Play returning subgames like Samurai Kirby as well as brand-new ones like Magolor’s Tome Trackers and Booming Blasters for in-game rewards. Hop online** to see global fun facts or compete against player records from all over the world in Samurai Kirby 100.
AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.review_text:
Port of a Wii game with some added extras. The core experience/main campaign is the same with main differences being the epilogue chapter post game, added mini games, and some quality of life changes.

No online multiplayer, local only

Still a fun game and one of the best side scrolling Kirby game in the franchise. If you haven’t played Kirby Forgotten Land, I’d consider getting that instead.

--List of changes:

1. Graphics. Updated visuals. Less pixelated. Runs at 60fps

2. Magolar Epilogue.

Additional chapter after you beat the game with new levels. You play as Magolar who is stripped of all his powers and on a journey to get them back. You gain them back through points you spend on a skill tree.

This chapter can be completed in 2 hours or so. Levels are more creative than original campaign especially the boss battles

3. Merry Magoland.

Can play compilation of mini-games across Kirby franchise.

Playing mini-games earn you stamps. Able to cash in stamps for masks which you can use in main campaign

4. Helper Magolar.

Assist mode (can turn on or off) during main campaign, picks you up when falling like Lakitu's in Mario Kart.

Also gives you free potions when notice you struggling.

Note: not available in bonus epilogue chapter

5. New copy abilities: Mech Kirby and Sand Kirby

6. New move sets for returning copy abilities

7. Dodge move added. Initiated when blocking like Smash Bros game

8. Can use items or backup ability

9. Able to use thumbstick instead of just D-pad (like wii version). Only able to move in 8 directions

AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.review_text:
Game is available on Xbox Gamepass.

Very Bioshock and Wolfenstien inspired. There’s even a underwater elevator segment where the protagonist mentions rapture

Linear first person shooter. You have an inplant in your left hand that does elemental and physical attacks/defense.

There is a slight open world segment like Gears of War 5 or God of War Ragnorok

Plot: Set in alternate post WWII where robot takes over the city in USSR. Most enemies are robot zombie mannequins, or mechanized ball machines with arms sticking out. Main character has frat boy edgy attitude, can be annoying at times. There are 2 endings. Dependent on a single choice you make at the end

--Gameplay:

Movement and gun handling feels very floaty. Not very engaging especially when guns have odd handling and recoil

Very melee focused in the beginning. Swings are way to wide and must focus on timing

Best aspect of combat is the powers plus gun combo when you’re able to chain it together

Powers are on a cooldown system. Able to have 3 on queue, but not able to remove shock for some reason

Enemy placement is bad. They just flood you with random enemies on screen. Not in a good horde mode kinda way, more like a noob player created level in Little Big Planet, Mario maker style game.

Enemies are bullet sponges at times. Can feel tedious at times finishing off enemies

No sprint. Must upgrade walk speed via skill tree

Aside from the sprint upgrade, skill tree gives you good buffs. Can re-spec at anytime . Need to upgrade at upgrade station where this horny robot tries to flirt with you

Loot resource based gameplay. Get resources after defeating enemies to upgrade skill at upgrade station. Need to collect schematics to upgrade weapons

There are environmental puzzle segments that change the pace a bit where you must use your powers to get to next section

Like most first person shooters, the 3d platforming is horrible

Manual save format at save stations. Checkpoints are okay, can be frustrating if you get gameover after taking out dozens of bullet sponge enemies just to be killed by one bringing you back 5-15mins

There are some glitches and sections where you need to soft reset the game.

Up to 15 second load times are present even on PS5

Overall okay game. Very Bioshock and Wolfenstien inspired but the gameplay is not very polished or well thought out. Could have used more development time in my opinion

Short version: I've really enjoyed this one. It's great if you're into fighters.
So, I picked this up as a casual fan of KoF. I've had a good time so far.
Graphics - I miss the old 2D sprites, but they did a good job with these this year. Way better than earlier 3D attempts. The characters are big, detailed, and have a lot of personality. Animation is top notch. The moves look great in action. Stages are really nice too.
Gameplay - I enjoy this game a lot. It's fast and responsive, and the systems work well. I come from Street Fighter, so I've been able to cobble together a few ideas. Online - I haven't been able to try this yet. Not sure how the community is.

AG2NAOAGWP6DHXTVIHYADL4AKTHQ.product_description: Explore a fantastical adventure world to defeat a bodybuilding dragon and his minions using real-life exercises. Two new accessories, Ring -Con and Leg Strap, measure your real-world actions and help turn them into in-game movements. AG2NAOAGWP6DHXTVIHYADL4AKTHQ.review_text:
Quick version: I've found this is actually a really fun way to game-ify my workout routine.
Longer: I got this on a whim when it was on sale. I can happily say this has been a good purchase. It's been a fun way to add some gaming to my workout...or add a workout to my gaming maybe?
The story isn't anything amazing, but at least they have it. It helps to have a story goal...I'm glad they have it.
Graphics and sound are fine. Again, nothing earth shattering, but they're nice and serviceable.
Gameplay is really fun. The game does a good job of easing you into the adventure. It starts pretty easy, and you can ramp it up as you'd like. My only wish is that it gave you more exercises up front...for the 1st few days, I was afraid I'd be doing the same 3 or 4 things each day. Not the case...you unlock more exercises as you progress.
The ring is responsive...I was afraid there'd be some disconnect with the ring/joycon/strap setup, but I haven't had any issues.
The game does a good job of showing and telling you tips for the exercises, as well as general health info. Tips for eating, stretching, breathing, drinking water...all welcome additions. And the game does a good job of encouraging you, too. New items, activities, and others...seems like there's always something to shoot for.
All in all, this seems like a good addition to my games AND my workout.
Thanks for reading!

AH5T76LCWLZVZICFCBXBY7KAGL3Q.product_description: Explore Metroid history with the Metroid dread: special Edition Bundle. In addition to a haunting steel game case, this Edition includes the game card, 5 high-quality art cards with key art for each 2D Metroid game and a 190-page art book spanning all 5 entries in the 2D Metroid saga.
AH5T76LCWLZVZICFCBXBY7KAGL3Q.review_text:
I was expecting good things from Metroid Dread. I didn't really know what to expect from this game though, since Nintendo haven't exactly treated Metroid with respect and integrity in recent years. (Looking at you Federation Force...)
However, I am delighted to say that this game absolutely exceeds ALL expectations. I've not beaten the game yet, but I'm willing to say this may be the single most perfect Metroid game Nintendo has ever given us. This game, unlike so many others in the last few years, gets a 10/10 from me, and this is high praise, as I am a very harsh critic.

The reason I'm able to give M:D such a high score is because it doesn't hold back in the slightest. If it's going to punch you, it's going to give you everything it has. Bosses are beautifully crafted and not one single boss has been too easy or lame. Every single one has challenged me, killed me, and forced me to think outside the box to beat them. Each death adds to your skill in ways most other games don't even touch on, even Samus Returns.

This game is built on the Samus Returns gameplay style. While I really enjoyed SR, M:D takes this engine to a new level. There are numerous quality of life improvements and tons of well thought out mechanics, much more so than SR. If Samus Returns were a food, it would be a hamburger, filling and delicious. If Metroid Dread were a meal, it would be a 3 course at Gordon Ramsay's restaurant. I mean this in every way. A hamburger you eat and you're done. M:D just keep coming and coming and coming and its amazing and terrifiying and beautifully crafted.

The sound design is so incredibly amazing. Rarely do I find myself in quiet rooms outside the safe area. The ambiance is superb. The soundtrack makes your heart race. When you're in danger, YOU ARE IN DANGER, and the audio beats right along with your poor overencumbered heart. The music and sound design makes this game feel like, "It's GOING," or, "It's HAPPENING!" Moments where you catch your breath are sweet sighs of relief, readying yourself to enter that doorway and get back in the fight or flight again. The only criticism I have on sound is that they reused the heat and lava warning sound from SR. That blaring alarm sounds so low quality next to all the other sounds. They also reused the parry sound, but that fits right in.

Art. Art is good. This game's got hella art. See the art. Very pretty, much gaze. The graphics, while not the best, still looks incredible. The stylization Nintendo is known for for making lower resolutions look outstanding is here. There are aliasing lines, but I can ignore those.

Enemies are much more varied than in SR. Each one has its own timing for parry, and some don't even have parry as an option. There seem to be enemy types that all areas abide by, but instead of how they did it in SR where new enemies were just recolors, every area has their own enemy models. Going to a new area and seeing new gnarly toothed floating things of rage is really cool and every zone has enemies that fit it's environment.

So far the story is phenomenal, and I rarely give stories high praise. I won't say much here because these are spoilers you won't want spoiled. Needless to say, Metroid type s*** is HAPPENING! This game has a lot of cannon. Metroid Fusion is important, being the last mainline 2D Metroid game in the series. Exploring the game later on after a certain event takes place (you'll know what I mean when you get there) is unbelievably cool and nostalgic. Beating my first... uh... certain.... boss... filled me with IMMENSE pride, like I showed it whos boss again and told it to sit tf back down. This story is very well crafted and I hate that I shant say anything more about it. 10/10. This game also has voice acting! And it's good!
Controls are tight. If you get hit, you are unable to move for a split second. The puzzles in this game are all well thought out and thought provoking. There were a couple spots where I thought I was softlocked but I was just being a big ol stupidhead. Being a veteran of Metroid games, I feel I have been able to find my way through the maze of the map fairly easily, but then again, I think the map design and room placement is also just really good.

If you like Metroid, have played a 2D Metroid in the past, and have played Samus Returns or Metroid Fusion, this game is for you.

Just a disclaimer for the weak of heart. This game is named Metroid Dread for a reason. This game will fill you with dread as you watch helplessly as you die. There are zones where you are powerless against the specific type of foe. I joked with one of my friends that this game could have also been called Metroid: Scary or Metroid: AAAH!

I find it helps to tell the enemies "please sir do not hurt me for I am just a wee baby."
Enjoy this masterpiece!
